common_name: Sunred Nectarine (Ultra-Low-Chill Gulf South Nectarine, Florida-Texas Nectarine)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter to early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep fertile soil in full sun; Sunred has an extraordinarily low chill requirement of approximately 150–200 hours below 45°F — the lowest of any widely available named nectarine — making it the standard recommended nectarine for the Gulf South, central and south Texas, and the southern half of Florida where winter temperatures are too mild for standard nectarines; self-fertile, no cross-pollinator needed.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Sunred is self-fertile; thin fruits to 6 inches after natural drop; Sunred ripens early (May in zone 8–9, one of the earliest nectarines available), producing medium-sized, bright red, freestone fruits with yellow flesh and good sweet-tart flavor — the first fresh nectarines of the season in Gulf Coast markets. Sunred nectarine is the primary low-chill nectarine for Gulf South and Florida gardening, selected for its exceptionally low chill hour requirement (approximately 150–200 hours) that allows it to bloom and fruit reliably in the mild winters of Houston, New Orleans, Tampa, and similar Gulf Coast cities where the 400–600 chill hours required by standard California nectarines are rarely accumulated in a typical year. The variety was developed through University of Florida and related southern breeding programs specifically to fill the gap in the Gulf South stone fruit palette, where peaches had low-chill options (Flordaking, Florida Crest) but the nectarine category was essentially absent for warm-climate gardeners. Sunred ripens in May in zone 8–9 — among the very earliest of any nectarine — providing a fresh stone fruit harvest at a time when nothing else is ripe, giving it a clear market window at Gulf Coast farmers' markets and specialty farm stands. The fruits are medium-sized, bright red, with yellow freestone flesh and a pleasant sweet-tart nectarine flavor that, while not matching the complex richness of premium California nectarines in cool growing conditions, is excellent for the region.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Low-Chill Fruits for Texas
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Peach and Nectarine Varieties

common_name: Fantasia Nectarine (Large-Fruited California Standard Nectarine, Freestone Commercial Nectarine)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep fertile soil in full sun; Fantasia requires approximately 400–500 chill hours and is self-fertile; best adapted to the warm interior valleys of California, the Pacific Northwest, and zones 6–9 where summer heat ripens fruit fully; in the humid Southeast, standard fungicide spray programs for brown rot are required as nectarines' smooth skin (unlike peach fuzz) allows brown rot spores to penetrate more readily.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Fantasia is self-fertile; thin to 5–6 inches in spring for maximum fruit size; Fantasia ripens mid-to-late season (late July to mid-August in California) producing very large, red to yellow-red blushed freestone nectarines with firm, yellow flesh and excellent sweet-tart flavor; outstanding for fresh eating and fruit stands. Fantasia nectarine is one of the most widely planted mid-season nectarine varieties for commercial and home production in California and the warmer western states, developed by Zaiger Genetics and released in the 1970s as part of the wave of nectarine variety improvement that transformed nectarine from a specialty fruit to a mainstream commercial crop in California's San Joaquin Valley. The variety's primary attributes — very large fruit size (among the largest freestone nectarines available), excellent red skin color with bright yellow background, firm flesh for shipping and handling, and a well-balanced sweet-tart flavor — made it ideal for the fresh market where visual appeal and size command premium prices. Fantasia consistently produces one of the largest individual fruits of any nectarine variety, with well-colored red-over-yellow skin that is visually very attractive, and the freestone flesh separates cleanly from the pit at full maturity — a practical advantage for home eating and cooking. The variety's moderate chill requirement (400–500 hours) makes it reliable in zone 7–9 climates beyond California, including the Pacific Northwest and some parts of the Southeast.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Nectarine Varieties
Washington State University Extension — Stone Fruit Production

common_name: Goldmine Nectarine (White-Fleshed Sweet Nectarine, New Zealand White Nectarine)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep fertile soil in full sun; Goldmine requires approximately 500 chill hours and is self-fertile; it is the most widely planted white-fleshed nectarine in home gardens in the western United States, prized for a sweeter, less acidic flavor profile than yellow-fleshed nectarines — white-fleshed stone fruits have lower acidity and higher sugar, giving them a distinctly floral, very sweet flavor character.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic fertile soil in full sun; Goldmine is self-fertile; thin to 5–6 inches; Goldmine ripens mid-season (late June to mid-July in California) producing medium-sized, reddish-white to pale red blushed nectarines with cream-white flesh and a very sweet, low-acid flavor with floral aromatic notes — quite different from yellow-fleshed varieties and appealing to those who find standard nectarines too tart. Goldmine nectarine is a New Zealand variety that became widely grown in California and Australia as the premier white-fleshed nectarine for home orchards — belonging to the white-fleshed stone fruit category that is vastly more popular in Asia (Japan, China, Korea) and is now gaining appreciation among Western fruit connoisseurs for its distinctly different flavor profile: lower acidity, higher sugar concentration, and floral aromatic notes that distinguish white nectarines from the familiar sweet-tart profile of yellow-fleshed varieties. White nectarines like Goldmine are at their best consumed very ripe — the flesh is tender and can bruise easily, making them poorly suited to commercial shipping but excellent for home-grown fresh consumption. At peak ripeness, Goldmine is exceptionally sweet and fragrant with honeyed floral notes, and the white flesh is tender, melting, and extraordinarily juicy — a texture and flavor profile that many white-flesh enthusiasts consider superior for fresh eating to even the best yellow nectarines. Best in zone 7–9 climates with warm, dry summers for ripening.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — White-Fleshed Nectarines

common_name: Cox's Orange Pippin Apple (World's Finest Eating Apple, English Dessert Apple Standard)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun in a sheltered location with good air circulation; Cox's Orange Pippin requires approximately 800–900 chill hours and cross-pollination from a compatible variety (Bramley's Seedling, Worcester Pearmain, or Egremont Russet); it is challenging to grow in the humid eastern United States (susceptible to apple scab, mildew, and fire blight) but flourishes in the Pacific Northwest and UK maritime climate.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun with good air movement; Cox's Orange Pippin is highly susceptible to apple scab and powdery mildew in humid climates — a preventive spray program or planting in the dry Pacific Northwest is strongly recommended; it requires a cross-pollinator; harvest in October when fully colored and giving slightly to pressure, then store 4–6 weeks to allow flavor to fully develop. Cox's Orange Pippin is the variety most consistently named by apple connoisseurs worldwide as the finest-flavored dessert apple in existence — a small to medium English heirloom discovered as a seedling by retired brewer Richard Cox in his garden at Colnbrook Lawn in Slough, Berkshire, England around 1825, and distributed commercially from 1850 onward. The flavor at peak ripeness is extraordinarily complex and rich by apple standards: intense, spicy-sweet, aromatic, with distinct notes of pear, mango, honey, and fresh nuts that no other apple variety precisely replicates — and which Cox's defenders argue no modern commercial variety (Honeycrisp, Gala, Fuji) can equal in flavor depth and complexity. Cox's became the dominant apple of British gardens and orchards for 150 years and remains the benchmark of the English fresh-eating apple tradition. In the United States, Cox's is most successfully grown in the maritime Pacific Northwest (Oregon and Washington's western valleys, similar in climate to England) where the cool, moist summers allow the apple to develop its characteristic complex flavor without disease pressure overwhelming the susceptible trees. In the humid eastern states, disease resistance is a significant challenge that requires diligent management.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Cox's Orange Pippin
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Heirloom Apple Varieties
Oregon State University Extension — Apple Varieties for the Pacific Northwest

common_name: Newtown Pippin Apple (Albemarle Pippin, Thomas Jefferson's Apple, First American Exported Apple)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, deep, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Newtown Pippin requires approximately 800 chill hours and cross-pollination from a compatible variety (Cortland, Winesap, or Golden Delicious); best adapted to zones 5–8; the variety produces its finest quality in climates with cool summer nights and good light — the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia and the Pacific Northwest valleys produce exceptional Newtown Pippin fruit.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Newtown Pippin requires a cross-pollinator; the greenish-yellow to yellow fruit with some russet ripens very late (October–November) and benefits from 4–6 weeks of cold storage before eating, during which the flavor mellows, sweetens, and develops complex aromatic notes; an outstanding cider apple as well as a fresh eating variety. Newtown Pippin is one of the oldest American apple varieties in continuous cultivation, discovered as a chance seedling in the early 1700s in what was then the town of Newtown on Long Island, New York (now the neighborhood of Elmhurst in Queens), and is distinguished by a historical record rivaling any apple variety in the world. Benjamin Franklin shipped Newtown Pippin to England as early as the 1750s, where it was received with acclaim; it was later selected as the apple to be presented to Queen Victoria in the early 19th century — and became so popular in England that the British Parliament waived the apple import duty on American Newtown Pippins, the only American apple to receive such distinction. Thomas Jefferson planted Newtown Pippin at Monticello, where the variety is now called 'Albemarle Pippin' for its long association with Albemarle County, Virginia, and still grown in the restored Monticello orchard today. The fruit is medium-sized, greenish-yellow to yellowish with russet patches, with crisp, dense, moderately tart, richly flavored flesh that becomes complex and aromatic after storage — nutty, slightly spicy, and quite unlike most commercial varieties. An important cider variety as well.
sources: Cornell Cooperative Extension — Heirloom Apple Varieties
Monticello — Thomas Jefferson's Fruit Garden Documentation
Virginia Cooperative Extension — Apple Varieties for Virginia
